title: Add Areas of Parts
description: Find the total area of a composite figure by adding the areas of its non-overlapping parts.
condition: A figure is decomposed into two or more non-overlapping rectangles or parts with known areas.
response: Add the areas of the parts to find the total area.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a shape is split into rectangles of areas $20$ and $40$, then total area $= 20 + 40 = 60$.

title: Area from Completed Array
description: Find the area of a rectangle by counting the total number of unit squares in a completed array.
condition: A rectangular array is shown with each small square representing one square unit.
response: Count all unit squares (or determine rows × columns) to find total area.
label: Principle / Model
example: If an array has 4 rows and 5 columns of unit squares, then area = $4 \times 5 = 20$ square units.
notes: May involve completing missing grid lines first.

title: Area of a Rectangle by Multiplication
description: Find the area of a rectangle by multiplying its length by its width.
condition: A rectangle with given side lengths $l$ and $w$.
response: Compute $l \times w$.
label: Principle / Model
example: For a $6$ ft by $3$ ft rectangle: $6 \times 3 = 18$ square feet.
notes: Core area formula for rectangles. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Compare Areas with Same Units
description: Compare two areas measured in the same square units to determine if one is greater, less, or equal.
condition: Two shapes have areas expressed in the same square units.
response: Determine whether one area is greater than, less than, or equal to the other by comparing numerical values.
label: Principle / Model
example: If shape A has area 10 square inches and Ray’s shape has area 12 square inches, then 10 < 12, so shape A is less than Ray’s shape.

title: Compare Two Areas
description: Determine whether one area is greater than, less than, or equal to another area.
condition: Two numerical area values are given.
response: Compare the numbers and state greater than, less than, or equal.
label: Principle / Model
example: If one area is $20$ cm$^2$ and another is $18$ cm$^2$, then $20 > 18$, so it is greater than.

title: Count Unit Squares to Find Area
description: Determine the area of a figure by counting the number of unit squares that cover it.
condition: A figure drawn on a grid where each square represents 1 square unit.
response: Count all full unit squares covering the figure.
label: Production / Skill
example: If a shaded figure covers 16 unit squares, its area is $16$ square units.
notes: Often includes systematic counting strategies. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Decompose Composite Shape into Rectangles
description: Draw a line to split an irregular composite figure into two or more rectangles.
condition: Given a composite rectilinear figure with right angles.
response: Draw one or more straight horizontal or vertical lines to form rectangles.
label: Production / Skill
example: An L-shaped figure can be split into a $4 \times 2$ rectangle and a $4 \times 4$ rectangle by drawing a horizontal line at the الداخل corner.

title: Evaluate Multiplication Expression
description: Compute the value of a multiplication expression.
condition: Given a numerical multiplication expression (e.g., 4 × 10, 5 × 3).
response: Calculate the product.
label: Production / Skill

title: Identify Correct Area Representation
description: Determine whether a visual model correctly represents measuring area by covering a rectangle with equal-sized, non-overlapping units.
condition: A picture shows a rectangle covered (or not covered) by unit squares or equal parts.
response: State Yes if it fully covers without gaps/overlaps using equal units; otherwise state No.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a rectangle is completely tiled with equal squares and no gaps, respond "Yes." If pieces overlap or leave gaps, respond "No."

title: Identify Rectangle with Equivalent Area
description: Determine which rectangle has the same area as a given rectangle by comparing products of side lengths.
condition: Given a target rectangle’s side lengths and multiple candidate rectangles.
response: Compute each area and select the rectangle whose area matches the target.
label: Concept
example: Target area: $3 \times 4 = 12$. Option B: $6 \times 2 = 12$, so it matches.
notes: Response is constant per item (one correct choice).

title: Identify Rows and Columns in Array
description: Determine the number of rows and columns in a rectangular array.
condition: A rectangular grid or array is shown.
response: State the number of rows and the number of columns.
label: Production / Skill
example: In a grid with 3 horizontal rows and 5 vertical columns, the structure is 3 by 5.
notes: Students may miscount rows vs. columns.

title: Identify Shapes with a Given Area
description: Select shapes whose total number of unit squares equals a specified area.
condition: Multiple shapes composed of unit squares and a target area value.
response: Count unit squares in each shape and select those equal to the target area.
label: Principle / Model
example: If the target area is 7 square units, choose shapes that contain exactly 7 unit squares.
notes: Combines counting and comparison to a fixed target.

title: Identify Side Lengths of Decomposed Rectangles
description: Determine and label the length and width of each rectangle formed after decomposition.
condition: After decomposing a composite figure, side lengths are partially given or can be inferred from the diagram.
response: State the numerical length and width of each rectangle.
label: Production / Skill
example: If the total bottom length is $7$ cm and a right section is $3$ cm wide, the remaining left section is $7 - 3 = 4$ cm.
notes: Often requires subtracting known segments to find missing side lengths.

title: Interpret Expression from Area Model
description: Determine whether a numerical expression correctly represents the area shown in a rectangular area model.
condition: Given an area model partitioned into dimensions (e.g., 4 by (10 + 2)) and a candidate expression.
response: Judge whether the expression matches the total area (Yes or No).
label: Concept
example: For a 4 by (10 + 2) rectangle, $4 \times (10 + 2)$ correctly represents the area → Yes.
notes: Includes recognizing incorrect forms such as $2 \times 10 \times 4$.

title: Label Rectangle Dimensions from Model
description: Determine and label the length and width of rectangles represented in a grid model.
condition: Given a tiled rectangular model with total length and visible partitions.
response: Count or infer the side lengths and assign correct measurement labels.
label: Production / Skill
example: Total length is 12 ft, shaded portion is 10 ft, unshaded portion is 2 ft; height is 9 ft.
notes: Requires interpreting grid and measurement labels.

title: Measure Area Using Identical Units Without Gaps or Overlaps
description: Recognize that area must be measured using identical unit squares that fully cover a shape without gaps or overlaps.
condition: Given multiple visual models for covering a shape to measure area.
response: Select the model that uses equal-sized unit squares and completely covers the shape without gaps or overlaps.
label: Concept
example: Choose the diagram where a rectangle is tiled with equal 1-unit squares arranged in rows and columns with no spaces.

title: Opposite Sides of a Rectangle Are Equal
description: Recognize that opposite sides of a rectangle have equal lengths.
condition: A figure is identified as a rectangle with one side length given.
response: Label the opposite side with the same length.
label: Concept
example: If the top of a rectangle is 12 cm, then the bottom is also 12 cm.
notes: Response is constant in that the opposite side must be equal to the given measure.

title: Select Valid Area Expression
description: Identify which expressions correctly represent the area of a given rectangle.
condition: Multiple expressions are given for the area of a shown rectangle.
response: Select the expressions that correctly compute rows × columns or equivalent repeated addition.
label: Rule / Plan
example: For a 3-by-5 rectangle, valid expressions include $3 \times 5$ and $5 + 5 + 5$.
notes: Requires verifying structural match to array.

title: Shade a Rectangle with a Given Area
description: Create or identify a rectangle that has a specified area by arranging unit squares into equal rows and columns.
condition: Given a target area in square units and a grid.
response: Shade a rectangular arrangement of unit squares whose total equals the target area.
label: Principle / Model
example: If Ray’s shape has area 12 square inches, shade a $3 \times 4$ rectangle since $3 \times 4 = 12$.

title: State Area with Square Units
description: Express area answers using appropriate square units (e.g., square inches, square feet, square units).
condition: An area value has been computed for a rectangular region.
response: State the numerical result followed by the correct square unit label.
label: Rule / Plan
example: $81$ square feet; $15$ square inches; $48$ square units.
notes: Unit label depends on context provided in problem.

title: Subtract Area of Removed Part
description: To find the remaining area after a part is removed, subtract the area of the removed part from the original total area.
condition: Given the total area of a whole and the area of a part that is removed or covered.
response: Compute $\text{total area} - \text{part area}$.
label: Principle / Model
example: If total area is $72$ and removed area is $15$, remaining area $= 72 - 15 = 57$.
notes: Applies to cut-out or covered regions.

title: Use Distributive Property to Find Area
description: Use the distributive property to break apart one factor when finding the area of a rectangle.
condition: One side length of a rectangle is expressed as a sum (e.g., $a+b$) multiplied by another side length.
response: Rewrite $(a+b) \times c$ as $(a \times c) + (b \times c)$ to find total area.
label: Principle / Model
